resources.string: Asma al-Husna means \"the most beautiful names\" — the names by which Allah has described Himself in the Qur\'an and the Sunnah. Allah says: \"And to Allah belong the best names, so call upon Him by them\" (Qur\'an, Surah al-A\'raf 7:180). They are also mentioned in Surah Ta-Ha 20:8, Surah al-Isra 17:110, and Surah al-Hashr 59:22–24. To learn them is to know your Lord — His mercy, power, wisdom, and majesty.

Abu Hurayrah (may Allah be pleased with him) reported that the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) said: \"Allah has ninety-nine names, one hundred less one; whoever enumerates them will enter Paradise.\"\n\nReferences: Sahih al-Bukhari 2736 (Book of Conditions), also 6410 (Book of Invocations); Sahih Muslim 2677 (Book of Remembrance). The number ninety-nine does not limit Allah\'s names — other authentic reports mention names known only to Him — but these are singled out for this great reward.

The word ihsa (enumerating) is far more than counting. Scholars such as Imam al-Bukhari, al-Nawawi, and Ibn Hajar (in Fath al-Bari) explain it as: to memorize the names, to understand their meanings, to believe in them, and to act by what they call for. So knowing Ar-Razzaq (The Provider) grows your trust in His provision, and Al-Ghafur (The Ever-Forgiving) turns you toward repentance. The reward is tied to living by the names, not merely reciting them.
A Note on the Listed Names

The familiar list of the ninety-nine names is narrated in Jami\' at-Tirmidhi 3507. Many hadith scholars — including Ibn Hajar — hold that the specific list is an addition by a narrator (idraj) rather than the exact words of the Prophet (peace be upon him), even though every name in it is established in the Qur\'an and Sunnah. This is why some traditional compilations differ slightly in their wording.
Calling upon Allah by His Names

Allah invites us to call on Him by His names (Qur\'an 7:180). Choose the name that fits your need: Ya Rahman for mercy, Ya Razzaq for provision, Ya Ghafur for forgiveness, Ya Shafi for healing. The Prophet (peace be upon him) also taught that Allah has a Greatest Name (Ism al-A\'zam) by which, when He is asked, He answers — reported in Sunan Abi Dawud 1495, Jami\' at-Tirmidhi 3544, and Sunan Ibn Majah 3856.

• Make the names part of your remembrance (dhikr) morning and evening.
• Let them shape your character: show mercy because He is Ar-Rahman, forgive because He is Al-Ghafur, be fair because He is Al-'Adl, be patient because He is As-Sabur.
• In hardship, turn to the fitting name in du'a and place your trust in Al-Wakil.
• Teach the names to your family and children, so knowledge of Allah passes on.

Reflecting on the names deepens knowledge of Allah (ma\'rifah), awe of Him (khashyah), love, hope, and reliance (tawakkul). The heart grows steady, gratitude increases, and the worshipper draws nearer to the One being remembered. The names are a doorway: the more you understand them, the more your worship gains meaning.

The reports above are cited from Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, Jami\' at-Tirmidhi, Sunan Abi Dawud, and Sunan Ibn Majah. Hadith gradings and details are best confirmed with reliable scholars and the printed collections. For deeper study, return to trustworthy teachers and books of tafsir and hadith.
Choose how many times to recite, or enter your own.
Turn on reminders to be notified on time. Set Suhoor and Iftar to your own local times.
Tells you when it is time for Iftar
Show the tap counter on each name
Name of the day, Suhoor and Iftar reminders
Finish your meal before Suhoor ends and make your intention to fast.
It is time to break your fast — a blessed moment for accepted prayers.
